AI Risk Levels E: Determining AI Risk Levels of Applications
Version 25.8.2024

The following questions can help you assess the AI risk level of your application involving or relying upon AI. The AI risk level refers to the risks for your organisation (financial, operational, reputational and compliance risks), not for data subjects, other affected parties or the public. Of course, such risks to others have to be considered, too. In the case of risks to data subjects about whom you are processing personal data you may even be required to undertake a special risk assessment referred to as a data protection impact assessment. The AI Act, in turn, may require you to perform a fundamental rights impact assessment in certain cases. Note that only AI related risks are at issue, i.e. risks that relate to the particular characteristics of AI systems, such as their autonomy. The consequences of the AI risk classification needs to be decided by you. For example, you can provide that applications with a high AI risk need to undergo a more comprehensive AI risk assessment.
Step 1:
Are the AI risks of your application insignificant?
This is the case if one of these questions is answered with yes:
Explanation:
An approved tool with AI is used for executing a narrow, subordinate and schematic task and has proven to be reliable in doing so (we fully trust it)
Examples include OCR, biometric user authentication in a broadly used commercial product (e.g., mobile phone fingerprint reader).

Employees use an approved tool with AI that supports them in their personal daily tasks where its failure to do so correctly at worst results in their own personal loss of efficiency they are ready to, and will, deal with (no impact on others)
Examples include using an approved chatbot to brainstorm, create summaries of Internet content or improve your own writing, using an approved translation tool for translating text for you.

An approved tool  with AI is used for simulations, analyses and tests that can have no impact on real processes, systems or others and involve neither personal data, nor third party content nor secrets (except with the consent of these affected third parties)

Examples including analyzing production or market data for certain patterns, whereas any patterns found would be further investigated. If personal data of third parties and third party secrets or copyrighted or otherwise protected content is to be used, this category only applies if there is consent of these third parties to use their data or content.

A tool with AI is only used for testing whether it can fulfill an envisaged task, it is not provided with personal data, third-party content or secrets, is operated securely, and the output is kept confidential and not in any way used in the "real world" or only in parallel to productive systems and not relied upon.

Examples include using an LLM in a lab environment to test whether it is suitable for a new company service, using a security breach detector side by side with the existing security tools to find out whether it could further improve security.
Step 2:
Does your application involve high AI risks?
This is the case if one of these questions is answered with yes:
Explanation:
Will we train an AI model on third-party restricted content or non-public personal data (without consent)?

This intends to cover those model training activities where the legal basis for performing it is legitimate interest or similar grounds as opposed to consent or a contractual permission. It covers both pre-training and fine tuning. This only applies where the data used is either non-public personal data or legally protected third party content (e.g., copyrighted works) that is not publicly and freely available.
Will AI take decisions or control that can significantly impact us or others or will many fear so?

An example could be a system where AI is used to pre-select job candidates based on their CV or to assess creditworthiness. This risk category also covers use cases where a significant amount of people (e.g., 30 percent) fear that AI will take over control of aspects of their life that they consider important.
Will AI interact with a large number of people concerning very risky or confidential matters?

An example could be an AI chatbot that advises users on compliance or legal matters, or that provides medical or psychological advice. Another example would be a robo-advisor for investments. As opposed to that, a conversational agent using AI for customer support does normally not fall in this category. The category could also cover AI agents that are included in certain products, for example, a self-driving car. This question both covers the risk that the AI will provide inappropriate or incorrect output or that it will receive input that needs increased protection.
Will AI play an essential role in operating critical infrastructure for us or others?

This would include, for example, a system using AI to control a self-driving car, a manufacturing line or the security of a critical network. The AI function needs to play an essential role, e.g., the correct functioning to a large part depends on the correct functioning of the AI.
Does our use of AI bear an increased risk of significant negative media coverage ("shitstorm")?

This is about whether what you intend is likely to cause a shitstorm in social media or otherwise because the public would consider it a no go for your company, i.e. it takes into account the specific expectations that stakeholder have with regard to your company.
Does the application qualify as prohibited or high-risk under the EU AI Act?

Select this if your analysis comes to the conclusion that the use case is likely prohibited or high-risk under the EU AI Act (in the latter case unless the de-minimis-exemption applies for sure). It is not relevant whether you are a provider, deployer or another role, and it is not relevant whether the AI Act actually applies to you with regard to its geographic reach.
Can AI failure reasonably result in considerable financial losses or other damage to us or others?

While any system failure can usually result in damage to those who relied on it, we are looking at those where the failure of the AI component to operate correctly bears an increased probability of causing considerable financial or other harm to your company or others (including in particular customers). For instance, an AI system used in financial trading, healthcare diagnostics, or autonomous driving could cause significant financial loss, legal repercussions, or physical harm if it fails. Damage that will be covered by the company as opportunity costs (and will thus be fully cured) is not considerable. Another example could be an AI system where its failure could harm a large number of customers in a relevant (even non-financial) manner.
Can AI failure reasonably affect our ability to comply with applicable law/regulations in a serious manner?

This could be the case where we rely entirely on AI systems and their correct functioning to fulfil legal obligations (e.g. compliance with anti-money laundering obligations), and where compliance with these obligations is very important because, for example, we play an important role in the system (e.g. as a bank in relation to transactions).
Does the AI component require a significant investment or hold strategic importance for us?

This category aims at projects where it would cause the company significant financial, operational or reputational issues if it had to postpone, change or stop the project due to AI risks materialising that have not been assessed in the first place.
Do the AI aspects of the application otherwise indicate that there are high risks?
You have to ask yourself whether you see any other high financial, operational, reputational or compliance risks for your company due to the AI aspects of the application.
Step 3:
Does your application involve medium AI risks?
This is the case if one of these questions is answered with yes:
Explanation:
Will AI systematically support decisions that can significantly impact us or others?

Here, the AI system does not take a decision and does not have technical control, but will to have material influence on the decision making by humans. This only applies to applications where such support is intended, not coincidential and not rare (thus "systematically"). Examples could include systems that uses AI to analyse job applicants, to propose significant investments, to detect alleged compliance violations. The decisions do not have to involve individuals.
Will AI be used to systematically assess individuals (or their behaviour) for productive purposes?

This covers those cases that are not covered by the preceding category because there is no immediate decision involved or because a decision is not only supported, but made (with such decision, however, not triggering  a "high" risk). An example could be an AI function that analyses the usage of an online platform and causes the online platform to display specific content (e.g., a recommender system, a function that controls the content feed or targets advertisements).
Will AI interact with a large number external third parties on behalf of the organisation?

Examples could include an AI chatbot that provide customer service on the website of your company, and where people will reasonably expect that you accept responsibility for what the chatbot states (e.g., product or price information), even if there are some disclaimers.
Will the AI component of the application be an interesting target for manipulation or attacks?

This question assesses whether the AI component bears certain increased security risks. An AI system that handles sensitive data, financial transactions, or critical operations (e.g., fraud detection, cybersecurity) could be a high-value target for attackers.
Can AI failure reasonably result in financial losses or other damage to us or others?

While any system failure can usually result in damage to those who rely on it, we are looking at those where the failure of the AI component to operate correctly bears an increased probability of causing damage to your company or others. For instance, an AI system wrongly advising a customer may result in the company be required to pay a compensation.
Will AI be used to systematically process sensitive or third party content or secrets?

This question concerns the handling of confidential or otherwise restricted information by AI on a systematic basis, i.e. not only coincidentially and not as a rare event. Examples include processing medical records, legal documents, proprietary business data of others or copyright protected content that is available only under restrictive or paid license terms.
Will we sell a product or service where AI is an important part of it?

Here, AI is provided to third parties for their own, independent use, for example as part of a hardware or software product, or a service. However, AI must be an important feature of the product or service, such as in smart home devices, virtual assistants, or AI-driven analytics platforms, or play an important role for the correct functioning of the product or service, even if it is not marketed as such.
Will our AI training or application be based on data that will have to be kept anonymous?

This refers to cases (e.g., training of an AI model, a RAG implementation) where data is used in a form that could identify individuals or other third parties, but where we are at the same time under an obligation to ensure that users of the model or application cannot identify them.
Will we train an AI model on public and freely accessible personal data or third party content (without consent)?

This intends to cover those model training activities where the legal basis for performing it is legitimate interest or similar grounds as opposed to consent or a contractual permission. It covers both pre-training and fine tuning. This only applies to cases where all training data is publicly and freely accessible, e.g., available by way of an Internet crawler.
Will the quality of the AI output (correctness, bias etc.) be of high importance for success?

For applications like hiring processes, loan approvals, or medical diagnoses, the correctness and impartiality of AI outputs are important. However, do not consider the measures that you will implement to ensure quality, but rather how important they are.
Will our use of AI likely result in a considerable amount of employees or other people to have concerns?

This question evaluates the potential impact of adoption of the envisaged AI application with regard to its stakeholders, including the public (a considerable portion of a defined group of people would be 30% or more). Concerns may arise from job displacement, ethical implications, or transparency issues. Only consider reactions to the AI aspects of the application.
Will we be using AI or an AI provider that is unknown, intransparent or of questionable reputation?

Examples could be startups without relevant references, or known providers that have made more than usual negative headlines concerning issues that are relevant also for your project (e.g., issues concerning sub-standard security). Another example would be a provider who is not transparent about the technology and use of AI.
Will AI perform a function subject to industry-specific regulations or help ensure legal compliance ?

This question is to identify those applications that are either themselves subject to industry- or product-specific regulations (i.e. not only generic AI and non-AI regulations) or are used to ensure the company's compliance with industry- or product-specific regulations (e.g., tools for assessing legal compliance). Sectors like finance, healthcare, utility, and transportation are often subject to such regulations.
Do the AI aspects of the application otherwise indicate that there are medium risks?
You have to ask yourself whether you see any other medium financial, operational, reputational or compliance risks for your company due to the AI aspects of the application.
If none of the above applies, your application involves low AI risks.

GAIRA Light E: Overview: GAIRA is intended to allow an organization to make a holistic risk assessment of its AI applications. There is a normal version (see worksheet "GAIRA Comprehensive") and there is the "Light" version of GAIRA, on which worksheet you are right now. The "Light" version works best with small and medium sized and lower risk projects. Both versions of GAIRA are to be completed under the lead and the responsibility of the "business" or the application owner, whereas IT and information security experts and second line functions such as legal, compliance and the data protection officer should help. A typical approach is to have the application owner or project manager prepare the form and then have a discussion with the experts for going through the assessments, discussing and completing them. The ultimate decision is with the "business" or the application owner.
Status and date of risk assessment:
The implementation and use of the system.
Scope of assessment does not include:
The underlying cloud infrastructure (i.e. our tenant), including its security

Instruction: Fill out the below fields with a description of the application at issue. Also include information about the personal data that may be processed by the application, unless you do not have any such personal data. The information you enter is for information purposes. It will allow those who need to check the compliance and assess the risks of your application to better understand what you are doing and why.
Area in which the application is to be used:
Meetings of our relationship managers with our wealth management clients.

The bank wishes to implement a system that will use an AI solution to automatically record, transcribe and summarize all client-relationship and advisory meetings. Also, the system will automatically extract relevant information for the CRM system that is still missing or is newer. As part of this, the system will be used to identify potentials of net new assets of a client. The clients will be asked whether they agree to the use of the system with regard to their relationship. If so, the system will cover all interactions, whether via e-mail, phone or in person meetings. The data transcribed, summarized and extracted can be reviewed by the client's relationship manager. All recordings and other communications with the clients will be stored in our cloud tenant until it has been processed and then deleted.
The bank wishes to increase the quality and completeness of the data it maintains with regard to its clients, and wishes to increase the efficiency of its relationship managers.
GenAI techniques to be used (incl. model):
We use the DeltaPI-4 LLM, and CloudCo's service with which we can make available the LLM in our own CloudCo tenant.

CloudCo will be our provider who both runs our tenant (in a Swiss data center) and provides the AI services, through which we can make use of the DeltaPI-4 model. We have also used SoftCo as our implementation partner.
The input are the recordings of meetings and phone calls, the e-mails received from the client and other communications. We further allow the application to access the client's existing CRM records.
The output are the transcripts, the summaries and the data extracted, with all such output being imported into the CRM.
Period of time and frequency of use:
Daily, for the duration of the conversion project
Training or fine-tuning of the AI model, RAG1:
No training of the model; prompts are augmented by existing CRM records.
Categories of data subjects, if any:
Relationship managers, clients and people they refer to in their communications.
Categories of personal data, if any:
Content of meetings, content of communications (calls, e-mails, letters).
Other persons or groups that may be affected:
The output generated will be reviewed by the relationship manager.
Reporting to the application owner for analysis and remediation with the application engineers.
As per the Groups Information Security Policy.
Measures to control AI risks (other technical):
"Low temperature"; input data is deleted after three months after the extraction.
Measures to control AI risks (other organizational):

Contractual provisions for processing CID, for ensuring in-geo processing (our tenant), for applying Swiss data protection law, regarding purpose limitation and for ensuring in-geo processing (the AI services we wish to use); opt-out from content filtering; opt-out from abuse monitoring and human review; Verification that abuse monitoring is deactivated; contractual indemnities in case of IP claims due to the use of Azure OpenAI services; obtain client's prior consent; update privacy notice; right of access; testing; monitoring, including the retention of logs; banking secrecy waiver; training of relationship managers.
A pilot phase in Q2 2024 with a full roll-out in Q3 2024.
Higher quality of data and more data about clients, which will also result in better treatment of clients. Higher efficiency.
Is your application subject to the EU AI Act?

Instruction: The EU AI Act will govern, once the relevant provisions become applicable in the 6-36 months following August 1, 2024, a number of AI use cases, whether they take place in the EU or not (i.e. the AI Act has extra-territorial effect). The following section lets you determine whether the AI Act will apply to your use case, whether your use case is prohibited under the AI Act or regulated as a high-risk AI system, and whether you are subject to the AI Act and in which role. Note that even if you do not have a high-risk AI system, some regulations (mainly concerning transparency) apply.
The AI System or AI model
Do you make use of an automated component (e.g., program, IC) that generates relevant output based on input at least partially autonomous (i.e. based on rules not defined by human beings)?
Could your application be qualified as doing at least one of these activities listed in Art. 5 AI Act?
Could your application be qualified as doing at least one of these activities listed in Art. 6 AI Act?

Does the high-risk AI system nevertheless (i) pose no significant risk of harm (e.g., narrow procedural task, quality control of human activities or completed decision making) and (ii) does it not perform any profiling?

Are you creating an AI model  that displays significant generality and is capable of competently performing a wide range of distinct tasks that can be integrated into a variety of downstream systems or applications?
Whether you are subject to the AI Act as a "provider" or in a similar role
Will you have developped (yourself or through a third-party) the AI system or model, partially or fully?
Will you be the first one placing the AI system or the general-purpose AI model on the EU market?
Will you be the first one putting the AI system into service by yourself or by someone else in the EU?
Will you do either of the two foregoing activities under your own name or trademark?
Will you put your name or trademark on the high-risk AI system that is already on the EU market?
Will you make substantial modifications to the high-risk AI system that is already on the EU market?
Will you be using the AI system for a high-risk activity (as above), even though the AI system was neither intended to be used for such activity by the provider of the system nor itself is high-risk?

Is the AI system a safety component of your product, and will you (ii) place it on the EU market with your product under your own name or trademark or (ii) put it into service in the EU under your own name or trademarkt after the product has been placed on the market?
Although you are not a provider, will you still make the AI system available on the EU market?
Are you established in the EU and will you place the AI system on the EU market under the name or trademark of a third-party outside the EU?

Note that the AI Act intends to also cover the providers of those AI systems where the output is intended to be used in the EU. However, the AI Act has been drafted in a manner that excludes these cases. We have decided to not include this case here, but it may still apply, depending on the interpretation of the AI Act.
Whether you are subject to the AI Act as a "deployer"
Will you be using the AI system under your own authority?
Are you established or located in the EU?
Is the output produced by the AI system intended to be used in the EU?

Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) (only if personal data is processed)

Instruction: A data protection impact assessment or DPIA is required by the GDPR and Swiss Data Protection Act (DPA) if the processing of personal data is likely to bear a high risk of unwanted negative consequences for the individuals about whom you process personal data. The "high risk" is determined absent the mitigating measures you may take. Even if you manage to control all risks, you will have to do a DPIA if the risk of the AI solution you are planning to implement is inherently high. In the first part of this section it will be determined whether such an inherent high risk exists. If so, you will have to complete the second part of this section. The purpose is to identify the risks of your AI solution to the individuals affected and see whether any additional measures need to be implemented to control them. Unless you identify any high residual risks, you can continue. If you have high risks despite the countermeasures, you will have to discuss this with your 2nd line, as processing activities with a high residual risk usually have to be submitted to the data protection authority (under the GDPR and Swiss DPA). If you want to perform a DPIA with another tool, you may do so, of course. The below DPIA has been designed for simple cases.
Determine the need for a DPIA
Will we be extensively processing sensitive personal data11)?
Will we be systematically monitoring public areas on a large scale?

Is there a systematic, extensive evaluation of personal aspects of individuals based on AI or otherwise automated, which may have a legal or otherwise significant negative impact on these individuals?
Could wrong (including biased) output of the AI solution have a significant negative effect on individuals (not considering the measures we take to prevent this)?
Is the AI solution subject to an increased risk of attacks by third parties and could a successful attack have significant negative effects for individuals?

Is there another reason (including considering the above assessment) why the AI solution could provide for a high risk of negative effects for the individuals about whom we process data (not considering the mitigation measures we take)?
Recommendation whether to do a DPIA:
Your decision - will you do a DPIA (because you have to or because it in any event makes sense)?

In column "D" you should list the most important measures you have or plan to implement to prevent this from happening (e.g., the security measures you envisage, privacy notices, human oversight). Then select the probability with which you believe that the scenario could nevertheless (i.e. despite all the mitigating measures) happen in the time period of the DPIA (see below, by default three years)). In column "F" please describe the reasonably possible negative consequences for the data subjects and then rate their personal exposure to damage (not the exposure of the organisation), should the risk scenario occur. The system will calculate the residual risk, and you can provide a final comment on it (for instance why you believe it is particular high or low). Have the 2nd line comment on it (or help you if you).

The term "RAG" refers to a technique called "Retrieval Augmented Generation" and does not qualify as "training" an AI model. Rather, the AI system is in a first step asked to analyze the request, then formulate the additional information it needs in the form of a search request and then performs the search. The results of the search will then be fed to the AI system for responding to the initial request. In other words, the original instruction is "augmented" with additional knowledge to use for answering it.

The term "unsafe" countries refers to countries that are not considered having an adequate level of statutory data protection (from the perspective of the EEA, CH and UK; for the EEA, the list of "safe" countries includes all EEA member states and the countries with an "adequacy" decision, which are listed here: https://vischerlnk.com/3StIhBp; for Switzerland, the list is in "Anhang 1" of the Data Protection Ordinance: https://vischerlnk.com/3SHGJFe).

The term "confidential data" refers to either our own business secrets or business secrets of a 3rd party that we have received under an non-disclosure agreement or other confidentiality undertaking. The term "legally protected data" refers to content that is the intellectual property of a third party and has been licensed to us under certain conditions. We have to make sure that we comply with these conditions, including in particular when using such data as input to an AI system.

If you do not know the answer to this, you will have to ask your 2nd line functions that deal with this, for example, your legal department or the data protection officer. They may have to review these contracts etc. for you and let you know the answer you can include here.

This refers on the one hand to contractual measures, such as the conclusion of the EU Standard Contractual Clauses (EU SCC) with the party abroad (e.g., the provider), and on the other hand us checking whether there is any relevant risk of our data becoming subject to problematic lawful access by authorities in the country of the recipient. For "safe" countries (see footnote 2) this is not necessary. This is typically an issue you will have to check with your 2nd line, e.g., the DPO or legal department.

If we want to use a provider for processing data that is subject to official or professional secrecy, additional precautions need to be taken. In particular, the provider contract will need special clauses (e.g., containing a "defend your data" provision and a proper confidentiality undertaking for the provider, its employees and for beyond the term of the contract). Providers often provide special amendments for this purpose upon request. Furthermore, technical and organizational measures will have to implemented to protect the data against both misuse and foreign lawful access. It may also be necessary to perform a foreign lawful access risk assessment (FLARA). You will have to ask your 2nd line to provide you guidance on this, unless you already know.

The "records of processing activities" (ROPA) is an inventory list of all activities that involve processing personal data at the organisation. Depending on the organisation, it is a requirement under data protection law.

Art. 50 EU AI Act contains various transparency obligations for both providers and deployers of AI systems that apply even if the AI system is not considered "high-risk" (and, thus, subject to separate obligations). They are listed in the table in the separate worksheet "AI Act" (towards to the end).

There are various reasons why organisations should or will want to log the use of the AI solution. First, data protection act may require audit trails to detect and track improper processing of personal data (e.g., Art. 4 Swiss Data Protection Ordinance). Second, the AI Act requires that high-risk AI systems log their activities (including input/output) and that deployers retain it for six months. Third, logging of input and output may be necessary for evidentiary purposes or because regulations require that companies document their activities and use of certain tools (namely for the purpose of regulatory investigations).

This refers to the content mentioned also in footnote 3), which is either business secrets of third parties or intellectual property of a third party. Here, the question aims at finding out whether we have made any commitments to those who have entrusted their business secrets or intellectual property to us under the expectation or condition that we will not use it for AI solutions such as the present one. That said, most contracts will not contain any such restrictions.

With the term "sensitive personal data" we mean personal data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious or philosophical beliefs, or trade union membership, and the processing of genetic data, biometric data for the purpose of uniquely identifying a natural person, data concerning health or data concerning a natural person’s sex life or sexual orientation, other personal data about the person's intimate sphere, personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences or related security measures as well as data on social security measures. These categories of personal data are subject to special protections under the GDPR and Swiss data protection law. The term "profiling" means any automated processing of personal data consisting of the use of personal data to evaluate certain personal aspects relating to a natural person, in particular to analyse or predict aspects concerning that natural person’s performance at work, economic situation, health, personal preferences, interests, reliability, behaviour, location or movements.

A legal basis is only required under the GDPR or, in under Swiss law, where an institution is processing data in its capacity as a federal or cantonal authority (or, also under Swisslaw, if a processing activity is in breach of certain principles of data protection). Under the GDPR, a legal basis for processing normal data could be the need to perform a contract with the person at issue, its consent, a legal obligation or an overriding legitimate interest.

Overview: GAIRA is intended to allow an organization to make a holistic risk assessment of its generative AI applications. GAIRA Comprehensive is suited for projects that likely have a high risk (see the Worksheet for "GAIRA Light" for an assessment of whether a project likely has a high risk). GAIRA is to be completed under the lead and the responsibility of the "business" or the application owner, whereas IT and information security experts and second line functions such as legal, compliance and the data protection officer should help (comparable to a data protection impact assessment, which is actually included in GAIRA). One typical approach is to have the application owner or project manager prepare the form and then have a workshop with all stakeholders for going through the assessments, discussing and completing them. The ultimate decision is with the "business" or the application owner. In total, there a five steps to do. An instruction is provided for each step below.

Area in which the application is to be used:
Meetings of our relationship managers with our wealth management clients.

The bank wishes to implement a system that will use an AI solution to automatically record, transcribe and summarize all client-relationship and advisory meetings. Also, the system will automatically extract relevant information for the CRM system that is still missing or is newer. As part of this, the system will be used to identify potentials of net new assets of a client. The clients will be asked whether they agree to the use of the system with regard to their relationship. If so, the system will cover all interactions, whether via e-mail, phone or in person meetings. The data transcribed, summarized and extracted can be reviewed by the client's relationship manager. All recordings and other communications with the clients will be stored in our cloud tenant until it has been processed and then deleted.
The bank wishes to increase the quality and completeness of the data it maintains with regard to its clients, and wishes to increase the efficiency of its relationship managers.

Why the data processing is necessary or there is no less intrusive approach from the point of view of the data subjects:

The alternative to the application would be to have the various communications and meetings documented and information extracted by support staff. We expect this to not only result in additional human exposure (wealth management clients usually do not want their conversations to be listened to by other people) but also more errors. With the system, we believe we can achieve a level of quality and completeness of the documentation that we would otherwise not be able to have.
Why data processing is adheres to data minimization, is limited in time to what is necessary, and otherwise proportionate:

The recordings will be deleted once they are no longer required, and the information generated and extracted by the system will only be used to support the relationship manager in their work. There will be no automatic decisions, including for purposes of creditworthiness, and all information can be reviewed and changed by the relationship manager. We, therefore, believe that the application is proportionate. It essentially improves an existing processing of personal data.

Instruction: This step 4 is the core of GAIRA. Your task is, together with other stakeholders and subject matter experts (such as from IT, legal, compliance, security, risk and the business) go through the below list and prepared risk scenarios. For each risk scenario, consider whether it could happen to your application and what measures you have undertaken or plan to undertake to prevent this from happening; list them in column D with bullets. You can also mention other factors that will help to prevent the risk scenario from occurring. In column E you then have to select how probable the risk scenario is in your case, given the measures and circumstances in column D. Once you have selected this, think of the possible impact if the risk scenario were to nevertheless materialize. Think of reasonably possible negative consequences for the organization and list them (using bullets) in column F. Then assess the exposure of the organization, in the hypothetical case that the risk scenario occurs (which you may believe is unlikely). The exposure depends on the magnitute of damage the risk scenario could cause and its probability. Do this assessment for all categories of negative consequences (even where they overlap), e.g., with regard to the financial harm the risk scenario could create to the organization, reputational issues, regulatory action (provided you are subject to regulatory oversight - if not, just select N/A) and potential criminal action, if any (if none is reasonably possible, select N/A). After you have assessed the risks for the organization, you have to assess the risks for the individuals that may be negatively affected by the risk scenario (e.g., the people about whom you process data). Rate their exposure (taking into consideration any potential harm, even if not physical or monetary) in the same manner as you did before for the organization. Finally, you can use the last columns to provide for any comments (such as assumptions you have made in the risk assessments or points that still need to be clarified), information about the risk owner and how the risk is handled (e.g., whether it is accepted, further steps are taken, etc.). When doing this assessment, you will likely conclude that additional measures are necessary to mitigate the probability of the risk scenario occurring or to mitigate the negative consequences. If this happens, add the measures in the table of step 2, and do the risk assessment assuming that the measure has been implemented. If a particular risk scenario has an only theoretical probability of occuring, you do not need to assess it. Just select N/A as the probility of occurence in column E and consider providing a brief explanation. If you want to add risk scenarios, there are empty lines in each section.

Instruction: The EU AI Act will govern, once the relevant provisions become applicable in the 6-36 months following August 1, 2024, a number of AI use cases, whether they take place in the EU or not (i.e. the AI Act has extra-territorial effect). Note that even if you do not have a high-risk AI system, some regulations (mainly concerning transparency) apply. The most important obligations are shown at the end of the page.

AI to identify or analyze people based on their features or behaviour?

Will your application use AI to process people's faces, voices, or other personal features to recognize or identify them (on images, in public spaces, etc.), to guess how they feel or what they want to do, to categorize them (especially about sensitive topics like race or beliefs) based on their behavior or personal traits, or to collect or create images or recordings of people without them knowing?

AI to assess people at work or in education or influence people unknowingly?

Could it be perceived that your application uses AI to influence or manipulate people without them realizing it, to take advantage of people who might not fully understand what's happening (like children or vulnerable individuals), or do you use AI to assess or monitor people at work or in education based on their behavior or personal traits?

Use AI for decisions or functions impacting people's lives or safety?

Will you use AI to make important decisions that could affect people's lives, rights, or access to essential services—like deciding if they get a job, can go to school, receive healthcare, get a loan or insurance, are involved in legal, criminal, or immigration matters, or is it used by law enforcement or judicial authorities, or is it used as a safety component in products regulated for safety (like medical devices, toys, machinery, or vehicles), or in critical areas where failures could cause significant harm (like utilities, transportation, or emergency services)?

Use AI to create content appearing human but isn't?

Will you use AI in a way so that people could erroneously believe it or the content (like text, images, audio, or video) it creates is human or real?

Description of Application (values taken from main page)
Area in which the application is to be used:
Meetings of our relationship managers with our wealth management clients.

The bank wishes to implement a system that will use an AI solution to automatically record, transcribe and summarize all client-relationship and advisory meetings. Also, the system will automatically extract relevant information for the CRM system that is still missing or is newer. As part of this, the system will be used to identify potentials of net new assets of a client. The clients will be asked whether they agree to the use of the system with regard to their relationship. If so, the system will cover all interactions, whether via e-mail, phone or in person meetings. The data transcribed, summarized and extracted can be reviewed by the client's relationship manager. All recordings and other communications with the clients will be stored in our cloud tenant until it has been processed and then deleted.
The bank wishes to increase the quality and completeness of the data it maintains with regard to its clients, and wishes to increase the efficiency of its relationship managers.
GenAI techniques to be used (incl. model):
We use the DeltaPI-4 LLM, and CloudCo's service with which we can make available the LLM in our own CloudCo tenant.

CloudCo will be our provider who both runs our tenant (in a Swiss data center) and provides the AI services, through which we can make use of the DeltaPI-4 model. We have also used SoftCo as our implementation partner.
The input are the recordings of meetings and phone calls, the e-mails received from the client and other communications. We further allow the application to access the client's existing CRM records.
The output are the transcripts, the summaries and the data extracted, with all such output being imported into the CRM.
Period of time and frequency of use:
No training of the model; prompts are augmented by existing CRM records.
Categories of data subjects, if any:

Instruction: This step 3 is the compliance check. It is only a basic compliance check to give you an indication of areas where you may have an issue and will have to a deep dive. For each requirement, choose whether you believe that it is fulfilled (or "covered"). If you conclude that this is not yet the case, but can be achieved with a further measure (e.g., conclusion of a contract, issuing a policy), then include the measure in step 2 of the main GAIRA worksheet. Until that has happened, select "Not yet" as a value. If a requirement is and will not be fulfilled, select "No". You can add further comments. Column G is "only" used to indicate which requirement is likely relevant or not for your application, based on the selection made in step 2 above. You have two options: You can use the below selector to have those requirements faded out that are likely not relevant to your application. Fade-out questions that are unlikely relevant as per the answers above:
Adequate data processing agreement with all relevant providers are in place

Most providers offer such "DPAs", as provided for by data protection law (e.g., Art. 28 GDPR, Art. 8 CH-DPA). A DPA requires the processor to act under instruction of the customer and provide for adequate data security.
Collection and use of personal data has a legal basis or justification insofar required

Some data protection laws require that you have a legal basis before collecting or otherwise using personal data. This could be consent, a contract to perform, a legal obligation or, where permitted, a legitimate interest.
Collection and use of personal data is fair
Consider this requirement from the perspective of the individuals about whom you process personal data.
Collection and use of personal data is known or transparent to affected individuals and covered in the privacy notice insofar required

Can the individuals affected (if interested) know that you are processing their personal data? Do you make your collection and use of their data recognizable? Is there a privacy notice that covers the application?
Collection and use of data is limited to the purposes for which the data has been collected (or compatible purposes)

Is it ensured that personal data will only be collected and used in this manner? Does any provider reserve the right to use your data also for its own purposes, and if so, is this justified? Is there a "secondary use" of data, and if so, is it justified?
Necessary correctness and completeness of the input data is ensured
Have you made sure that all input data is accurate in view of its purpose? Are there any measures to ensure so?
Necessary correctness an completeness of the output data is ensured
Have you made sure that all output data is accurate in view of its purpose? Are there any measures to ensure so?
Principle of "need to know" followed where granting access to personal data or secret data

Do only those have access to the data at issue that need it for their work? Will the selection of the data be limited to what is necessary? Is this also true for data that is to be disclosed? Are the recipients all "need to know"?
The collection and use of personal data is limited to what is necessary

Is it ensured that only the minimum data necessary in view of the purposes is being collected? Will the application only do with the data what is necessary for the purpose for which it has been created?
The input data is limited to what is relevant and necessary
Will only relevant and necessary data be provided to the AI system for processing?
Personal data and secret data is deleted or anonymized when no longer used
Will the data at issue be kept only as long as necessary and deleted/anonymized thereafter? Will access to it be restricted where this is reasonably possible?
Compliance with data subject rights ensured
They include the access right, the right to have data corrected, the right to object and restrict processing, the right to deletion, the right to withdraw consent and the data portability right.
Conditions for international transfers are complied with

Transfers of personal data to countries without an adequate level of data protection require safeguards (e.g., EU SCC), unless the organization can rely on an exception (such as explicit consent, contract performance).
Data sharing and joint controller agreements are in place insofar necessary
Where data is being shared with others or controlled with others, agreements between the parties involved may be necessary or recommended.
Rules for automated individual decisions are complied with insofar applicable
Fully automated decisions concerning individuals are restricted under some data protection laws and may trigger the right have decisions reviewed by humans.
Individuals are informed of our use of AI systems

For example, when they are interacting with an AI system, when material decisions relating to them are undertaken by an AI system or when content that could mislead them (e.g., deep fakes) has been create by an AI system. In some cases, this is mandatory under applicable AI regulations (e.g., EU AI Act for certain systems).
We have made sure that our use of AI does not result in a (prohibited) discrimination of individuals (e.g., ensuring that decisions are not relied upon biased models).
Proper confidentiality and "defend-your-data" clauses are in place with those third parties who get access to secret data
A confidentiality obligation (e.g., NDA) should be agreed with those who get access (including the provider), with an obligation to defend such data against any form of lawful access.
Foreign lawful access risks assessed insofar necessary and necessary measures have been taken

For some countries and some data, it may be necessary to assess the risk of access or seizure of such data by the local authorities in those countries. To keep the risk acceptable, special measures may be necessary such as limiting operator access, encrypting data or having it stored in defined jurisdictions that offer protection.
Necessary secrecy waivers are in place
Depending on who may get access to the data at issue, it may be necessary to obtain waiver from the owner of the secret.
An adequate level of data security is ensured, including business continuity; we have done a risk assessment and it did not result in any unacceptable risks
There must be adequate technical and organizational measures of security to protect the data against data breaches and ensure business continuity (e.g., with backups).
We have done a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) and it did not result in any high or otherwise unacceptable risks

A DPIA is required as per many data protection laws for high risk applications to ensure that the individuals about whom personal data is processed will not suffer unacceptable risks and to determine additional measures necessary.
We have done a general risk assessment of the application and it did not result in any unacceptable risks
The general risk assessment covers, in addition to a DPIA and information security risk assessment, other aspects such as AI, operational, commercial, compliance and reputational risks.
We understand the instructions and restrictions for use of the AI functions we use and comply with them
Where high-risk AI products are used, the understanding and complying with the instructions and information to come with them may be mandatory according to certain regulations (e.g., EU AI Act).
Instructions/training on how to properly set up, operate and maintain the application
This refers to the technical staff responsible for setting up, operating and maintaining the application.
End-user instructions/training on how to properly use the application
This refers to the users of the application.
We monitor how the application works, the effects it has and regularly re-evaluate all of this; we keep generated logs

In order to detect problems with the application and its AI systems, its operation has to be monitored and evaluated for risks. Where the application (and AI systems used for it) generate logs, they are to be retained sufficiently long. For high-risk AI systems this may be mandatory under certain regulations (e.g., EU AI Act), including the reporting of serious incidents (and eventually stopping the use of the AI system).
Necessary steps have been taken to use third party IP or content as applicable

Where the application involves any use or reliance on third party IP or other content, the organization has obtained the necessary rights (e.g., via licenses) unless it can rely on statutory exemptions).
Necessary steps have been taken to avoid deceiving or misleading third parties as applicable
This may involve quality controls on the output of the application, as well as informing users or the public about the use of AI.
Necessary steps have been taken to avoid breaching consumer protection laws as applicable
For example, such laws may require us to inform consumers of our use of AI or allow them to interact with humans instead of only AI systems
Necessary steps have been taken to avoid illegal coordination among market participants as applicable
Such coordination (e.g., regarding pricing) could, for example, result from the exchange of information (e.g., by jointly developing models for pricing).
Necessary steps have been taken to avoid misuse of a dominant position as applicable
This could relate to the use of terms & conditions in connection with the application, or how it is made available or under which conditions (e.g., permission for use of data).
The application has been properly tested in view of its criticality
The tests should cover both the quality of the output as well as the functionality of the application. Does it live up to its expectations?
Review of our contracts has revealed no conflict with the planned application
For example, contracts may contain restrictions on how to use a contract party's data.
The application, its implementation and its use is in line with applicable special regulations (including the EU AI Act, where relevant)

Where special regulations apply (e.g., of financial market supervisory authorities or by law) compliance with them has been checked and confirmed. This also includes AI regulations (e.g., the EU AI Act).
The application, its implementation and its use is in line with our internal policies and procedures
Where the application is governed by international policies and procedures, compliance with them has been checked and confirmed.
We have fulfilled our labour law obligations towards our employees.

For example, labour law may require consultation of employees or work councils, collecting bargaining agreements may restrict the use of applications that involve employee data. Employment law may also restricts surveillance of employees.
There are adequate quality control measures where the application's output could have consequences for individuals
For example, with regard to the accuracy and reliability of the output created by the application, or decisions or decision recommendations supposedly made by it.
We understand what our stakeholders expect from us with regard to our use of the application and comply with it

Stakeholders could be customers, employees, suppliers, shareholders, the public and others. They will usually have an expecation on how the organization "behaves" that is independent of the rules set forth by law. These "ethical" expectations will differ from organization to organization.
We have included the application in our Records of AI Activities.
The ROAIA table allows the organization to keep track of its activities in the AI area.
